---
title: "The Great Education Paradox"
subtitle: "200 million illiterate adults in the West — despite record spending on education"
author:
  - name: "[Authors TBD]"
    affiliation: Center for Global Development
date: "2026-04-20"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    toc: false
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
bibliography: references.bib
---

200 million adults in the rich Western world are functionally illiterate. We showed previously what this meant: They cannot read a sign, figure out where to put their Date of Birth on a form, compute calories on a box of cookies or read menus. Without some degree of literacy and numeracy, they are completely locked-out from participating in their societies.

Throughout the 20th century, similar numbers have been used to energize societies, teachers, and entire educational systems with a bellicose cry of a "War on Illiteracy." But here is what is difficult this time around: We really don't know anymore what such a war would imply.

To see where the problem lies, Figure 1, shows the growth in education budgets per capita from 1970 to 2020 in multiple OECD countries. Each grey line is a separate country (different lengths in the data series implies that they start and stop at different places) and the dark line is an average. What is clear is that education budgets *have dramatically increased even as adult illiteracy has barely budged–or become worse.* Think about that: At a time of the most exhilarating and rapid technological change that our societies have ever seen, and these are changes that *democratized knowledge*, put what the world knew at our fingertips, it became more and more costly to educate a child sufficiently so that they would be barely literate as an adult.

<!-- TODO: Figure 1 — education spending per capita 1970–2020, OECD countries. Source: OECD Education at a Glance. -->

The numbers are staggering:

* In the US, there are approximately **39 million** young adults between the ages of 16 and 24, of whom **30 million** are functionally literate — a share that fell from 86% in 2012 to 76% in 2023. They will have had roughly **$231,000** spent on their K–12 education (13 years × $17,800/year, OECD EAG 2023, USD PPP), which means that it is costing the U.S. approximately **$305,000** to educate one person to the point of functional literacy. In the early 1970s, that cost was approximately **$109,000** — less than a third of today's figure.
* In the UK, the picture is more complex. The UK spends roughly **$173,000** per student over K–12 (13 years × $13,300/year). Unlike the US, functional illiteracy among 16–24 year-olds has actually *fallen* — from 18% in 2012 to 12% in 2023 — so the cost per literate young adult has held roughly flat at **$196,000**. The UK pays less per student than the US and achieves better results among its youngest adults.
* In France, there are approximately **7 million** young adults aged 16–24, with cumulative K–12 spending of roughly **$156,000** per student. The functional illiteracy rate in this age group edged up from 12.5% in 2012 to 16% in 2023, pushing the cost per literate graduate from **$156,000** to **$186,000** — still well below the US figure but moving in the wrong direction.

*All figures: PIAAC age group 16–24, population-weighted. Spending from OECD Education at a Glance 2023, Table B1.1, USD PPP. Historical 1970s US values deflated via CPI (NCES Digest 2023, Table 236.55). See `01_scripts/08_education_cost.r` and `02_output/education_cost_stats.rds`.*

Economists call this the great productivity decline in education, and it remains one of the most underappreciated developments of the late 20th century: The West is becoming increasingly illiterate, not because governments are spending less money on education or because education is becoming less of a priority. No, they are becoming increasingly illiterate *despite* a substantial increase in what these countries spend on educating its citizens.

Let's dive a bit deeper into what is going on. Think about a population with just two age-groups: The young, and the old. Then, the extent of literacy in the population can be thought of as a weighted average of literacy among the young and among the old. That is, literacy in the population is: % of the population that is young \* literacy among the young \+ % of the population that is old \* literacy among the old. This way of thinking about it immediately allows us to decompose any changes in literacy into two components: The "share" component, which tells us how the % of the population in each category is changing over time, and the "age" component, which tells us the extent of literacy among the young and the old. For instance, if older people tend to be less literate and the population is becoming older over time, then literacy will fall, even if there is no change in literacy among the young or among the old. And in case there *are* these differences, we can go one step further and ask: If older people are less literate, is it because people tend to become less literate as they age, or is it because the older were educated at an earlier time, when perhaps educational attainment was a lot lower.

We ran the math and the answers are shown in the stacked bar graph of Figure 2. We consider only the population of functionally illiterate people in 2014 and 2023 (so, for every country, the total is 100 in 2014) and then we show how much of the increase is because (a) people grew older and their cognitive capacities declined versus (b) cognitive capacities changed *within* each age group. The specific numbers tend to be different, but they offer a similar picture. Declines in functional literacy reflect the fact that cognitive capacity declines with age, that older people are now a greater fraction of the population in most countries and that illiteracy seems to be rising within every age group as well. For instance:

<!-- TODO: Figure 2 — stacked bar decomposition (aging share vs. within-group change). Source: apc_cells.rds / new decomposition script. -->

* In the U.S. <!-- TODO: fill decomposition numbers for USA from APC regression outputs -->
* In the U.K. <!-- TODO: fill decomposition numbers for GBR from APC regression outputs -->
* In Korea <!-- TODO: fill decomposition numbers for KOR from APC regression outputs -->

Finally, we can now use these computations to project what would happen in 2050, even if we are able to fully halt the decline within every age group. The numbers should make anybody interested in the question of how we bring broad-based improvements to the citizenry of our country shudder. If our projections hold, within 25 years, the number of literate people in the labor force of these countries will shrink at an alarming rate. We are looking at <!-- TODO: XX --> in the U.S. and other examples.

<!-- TODO: 2050 projection requires UN World Population Prospects (fertility/mortality) combined with current illiteracy rates by age group. Separate data task. -->

In short, we are getting to the point that the post-literate Western society is now well within our line of vision. Western Civilization was always a good idea; its decay and unmanaged decline is sure to create chaos around the world.
